ground_truth.title: Vegetable Gardening | National Agricultural Library
ground_truth.main_content: A well-kept vegetable garden is a source not only of profit to the gardener but of pleasure to the entire family. New to vegetable gardening, or looking for ways to update your growing system? Learn about vegetable gardening through NAL collection items and selected external links to information. Easy Gardening resources from Texas A&M AgriLife Extension help homeowners plan a garden and grow vegetables, available in English and Spanish. The University of Minnesota Extension provides a guide to starting garden plants from seeds indoors, covering planning, planting and growing seeds, and moving seedlings outdoors. Cornell University offers growing guides with detailed descriptions and instructions, site and soil requirements, varieties, and more. Gardening History and Heirlooms section features historical documents including The Home Vegetable Garden (1906), The Small Vegetable Garden (1917), The City Home Garden (1924), and Diseases and Insects of Garden Vegetables (1931). Starting a vegetable garden can be achieved in six simple steps: Planning; site selection; soil preparation; planting your seeds; maintaining your garden; and harvesting. Container gardening enables growing food in window sills, kitchens, patios, balconies, wall gardens, and doorsteps. Companion planting takes advantage of natural relationships between different vegetable crops and other plants, improving productivity, deterring harmful pests, suppressing weeds, and attracting beneficial insects and pollinators. Recommended books on vegetable gardening include titles on garden planning, raised beds, pest management, and organic gardening methods. Ask your local Extension Service Agent about veggie gardening to learn how to start a vegetable garden, select and grow plants, identify and control garden pests, and more.
